coredata.dc:title: Enhanced anaerobic co-digestion under a magnetic field by a synergistic host–guest strategy: Focusing on accelerant, biogas yield, fertilization and coupled effect

coredata.dc:description: Carbon materials are widely used for direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) to promote CH4 yield in anaerobic digestion (AD). However, the addition of host–guest assembly carbon accelerant has not been reported. This work explores the facilitation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) immobilized in porous carbon (PC) as a host–guest accelerant in anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) under a magnetic field. The addition of CQDs and PC increased the biogas yield and TCOD removal efficiency by 30.1–38.6 % and 14.2–24.2 %, respectively, compared with control group (467.8 mL/g VS; 37.7 %). Fertilization efficiency of the optimal CQDs/PC group was 4.9 % and the excellent electronic exchange capability of CQDs (1.04 μmol e-/g) and PC (0.64 μmol e-/g) facilitated DIET. This approach helped in enriching anaerobic microorganisms and strengthened the thermal stability of the digestates. These findings provide new insights for improving AD performance using CQDs coupled PC.

head.ce:title: Enhanced anaerobic co-digestion under a magnetic field by a synergistic host–guest strategy: Focusing on accelerant, biogas yield, fertilization and coupled effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon materials are widely used for direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) to promote CH4 yield in anaerobic digestion (AD). However, the addition of host–guest assembly carbon accelerant has not been reported. This work explores the facilitation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) immobilized in porous carbon (PC) as a host–guest accelerant in anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) under a magnetic field. The addition of CQDs and PC increased the biogas yield and TCOD removal efficiency by 30.1–38.6 % and 14.2–24.2 %, respectively, compared with control group (467.8 mL/g VS; 37.7 %). Fertilization efficiency of the optimal CQDs/PC group was 4.9 % and the excellent electronic exchange capability of CQDs (1.04 μmol e-/g) and PC (0.64 μmol e-/g) facilitated DIET. This approach helped in enriching anaerobic microorganisms and strengthened the thermal stability of the digestates. These findings provide new insights for improving AD performance using CQDs coupled PC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: China is one of the largest agricultural countries worldwide and produces about 870 million tons of biomass waste annually [1]. Aloe, which is widely used for its medicinal and food value, produces large amounts of aloe rind which poses a threat to the environment [2]. Therefore, strategies enabling efficient re-use of biomass waste are required to reduce environmental pollution, with prime focus on biogas production and yield improvements in energy production efficiency [1]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a green, economical, and simple technology for converting biomass waste to methane and digestates [3]. However, AD is characterized by low biogas yields, unstable digestion, incomplete degradation of digested substrates, and a low utilization efficiency of digested waste products [4]. Therefore, improvement in AD is required to increase biogas yield, substrate biodegradation, and digestate utilization.

In recent years, performance of AD has improved, which can be largely attributed to changes in the mode of digestion (e.g., mono-digestion to co-digestion) [5], digestion environment (e.g., applied magnetic field) [6], and use of exogenous accelerant (e.g., carbon materials, other conductive materials, and compounds) [4,7]. For example, Zhang et al. [8] reported that at an organic load rate of 3 kg VS/(m3•d), the daily yield that can be achieved through anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) of food and kitchen waste increased by 24.7 % compared with that achieved by mono-digestion of food waste. Subsequently, AcoD reduced the inhibition of ammonia nitrogen and accumulation of volatile fatty acids (VFAs). Zhang et al. [9] showed that the production of methane (CH4) could be increased by 5.2–99.4 % by the AcoD of cattle manure, corn stover, or food waste compared with that achieved by mono-digestion, and that the AcoD subjugates carbon and nitrogen imbalance of AD. The biogas production during the AcoD of industrial by-products (vegetable oil) and sludge increased by 23.6–94.9 % compared with that obtained by the anaerobic mono-digestion of sludge, and the digestates could promote the growth of cress seeds [10]. The AcoD involves simultaneous digestion of two or more feedstocks, resulting in great system stability, better nutrient balance, improved buffering capacity, and a digestate with high nutritional value. Thus, the AcoD of various organic waste substrates is highly promising.

Magnetic fields (MF) have the potential to increase the CH4 yield by stimulating microorganisms growth, increasing enzymatic activity, and enhancing the breakdown of substrates in AD. For example, Qu et al. [11] found that a MF accelerated the decomposition of lignocellulose and increased CH4 concentration in the resultant biogas by 2.5 %. Zieliński et al. [12] found that the CH4 yield increased by 86.4 % in AD when MF was applied, which stimulated the growth of methanogenic bacteria and increased enzymatic activity. Jia et al. [6] and Abbas et al. [13] found that MF enhanced microbial activity and increased biogas yield by 7.3–45.0 %. However, an increased MF intensity can either kill or inhibit the microorganisms; therefore, an appropriate MF intensity must be applied [6]. Furthermore, MF failed to promote the interaction between microorganisms. Accelerants can enhance these interactions between anaerobic microorganisms [14]. Therefore, the addition of accelerants is highly desirable for optimizing AcoD performance.

Carbon materials (biochar, activated carbon, carbon quantum dots, graphite, graphene, etc.) are a widely used exogenous accelerant in AD owing to its rich functional groups, large specific surface area, and excellent electrical conductivity [4]. Additionally, these carbon materials derived from biomass through physical and chemical reactions and prepared at low temperatures retain many redox functional groups, which can serve as a medium for electron transfer, thus prompting the exchange of electrons between microorganisms and enhancing AD performance [3]. Furthermore, some carbon materials such as biochar and activated carbon with the porous structure (micropore, mesopore, and macropore) have a large specific surface area and pores, which facilitates microbial growth, thus improving the AD environment [15,16]. Moreover, carbon materials are excellent at facilitating interactions between anaerobic microorganisms, allowing for faster electron transfer and direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) process [16].

Among all carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of fluorescence carbon nanoparticles with fluorescence a particle size of less than 10 nm, possess excellent electrical conductivity that can enhance AD performance [17]. Li et al. [17] added of 5 mg/mL Zn-doped CQDs to AD with alcohol as substrate and increased the methane yield by 24.6 %; here, the DIET process was facilitated by the high conductivity of the Zn-doped CQDs. However, due to small size of CQDs, microorganisms fail to colonize and attach to them, which affects the stability of DIET during methanogenesis by anaerobic microorganisms [17]. To compensate for this, it was decided to use a host–guest assembly approach, while bio-based porous carbon (PC) was used as a guest material for microbial attachment. The introduction of shrimp shell-derived PC into the AD of food waste increased the abundance of Firmicutes and Methanobacterium in the system owing to the numerous growth sites provided by PC [15]. Sun et al. [16] added corn-straw-derived PC to AD, in which PC improved the microbial growth environment and provided attachment sites for microorganisms, resulting in the enrichment of Coriobacteriia, Methanosaeta, and Methanoculleus. Wang et al. [5] added acorn shell-derived PC to AcoD, and biogas production was enhanced by 7.1–37.5 %. This enhancement was probably due to the enrichment of anaerobic microorganisms by PC. Overall, the addition of CQDs and PC can be used to enhance DIET between anaerobic microorganisms and facilitate microbial attachment and proliferation. Therefore, host–guest accelerants with CQDs loaded PC were prepared to enhance the AD gas production performance.

Based on the suggestions mentioned above, it is hypothesized that host and guest assembly carbon accelerants (CQDs and PC) can enhance the performance of AcoD gas production under MF. Firstly, we investigated the impact of host–guest assembly carbon accelerants (carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and porous carbon (PC)) on anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) under magnetic field. The host–guest assembled carbon accelerator combines the excellent electron exchange ability of CQDs and the microbial enrichment ability of PC, which has a positive synergistic effect on the AcoD system. Secondly, we used aloe rind (AR) as a co-substrate for AcoD and as a raw material for the preparation of high-value CQDs and PC, achieving multiple and effective utilization of AR. Additionally, we systematically analyzed the effects of magnetic field and host–guest assembly carbon accelerants on methane production, substrate degradation, electrochemical characteristics, and microbial community, which lays the basis for elucidating the synergistic mechanism of magnetic field and carbon materials during AcoD process. Therefore, this work aimed to investigate the effects of MF and accelerants on AcoD by evaluating gas production (i.e., biogas and methane yields), AD process parameters (i.e., pH, TVFA, TA, TCOD, TS, and VS), digestate performance (i.e., fertilization efficiency, thermal stability, and electrochemical properties), and microbial community changes. Finally, it is proposed a mechanism to characterize the enhanced biogas production observed in AcoD using MF-coupled carbon accelerants.

Cattle feces (CF) were collected from a breeding farm in Xi'an, China. Aloe rind (AR) was obtained from a beverage factory in Yangling, China. The CF and AR treating process has been reported in previous work [2]. The anaerobic sludge (AS) was obtained from a wastewater treatment plant in Xi'an, China. The basic physicochemical properties of CF, AR, and AS are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and PC accelerants
ce:section.ce:para: The AR was used to produce CQDs and PC. The preparation process is shown in schematic diagram (Fig. 1 ). The AR was washed, dried, ground, and sieved to obtain AR powder. The AR powder was subjected to hydrothermal treatment (200 °C, 5 h) to obtain a solid–liquid mixture, which was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 5 min) to obtain a yellow liquid and a dark brown solid, respectively. The obtained yellow liquid was filtered, dialyzed and vacuum dried to obtain CQDs.

The obtained dark brown solid was mixed well with KOH and deionized (DI) water in a mass ratio of 1:4:20. The mixed solution was stirred (4 h), dried, and then placed in a quartz boat, and pyrolyzed (N2 atmosphere, 800℃, 2 h) in a tube furnace. The solid powder obtained by pyrolysis was washed through hydrochloric acid (HCl, 1 M), DI water to neutral and then dried at 105 °C to obtain PC. More details of the experiment can be found in previous work [18].

Based on previous work [18], different concentrations (0.8, 1.0, 1.2, and 1.4 wt.%) of PC solutions were prepared. Subsequently, according to the optimal concentration of CQDs in the previous work [19], 1.44 wt.% CQDs (based on 100 g) were added to 100 mL of the above solution. The mixture was stirred for 4 h, sonicated for 30 min, and then dried at 85 °C (Fig. 1). The final accelerant was marked as CQDs/PC(x), where × represents the PC mass percentage, where the host material were CQDs with excellent electron exchange capacity and the guest material was PC that can be enriched for microorganisms.

The batch MF supported AcoD (AcoDMF) experimental apparatus was mainly composed of three parts: AcoD reactor, biogas collecting bottle, and water collecting bottle. The 500 mL (working volume: 400 mL) jar was used as the AcoD reactor and the remaining top space to prevent the overflow of the digestate. The AcoD reactor was applied with MF. The biogas collecting bottle and the water collecting bottle were both 500 mL conical flasks. The volume of water in the water collecting bottle was measured to obtain the biogas production for the day. All experimental apparatus were joined with rubber and glass tubes. The co-substrate (7.5 wt.% of CF and 22.5 wt.% of AR) and inoculum (70 wt.% of AS) were added to the reactor according to the previous works [13]. To clarify the role of accelerants on the AcoDMF, six experimental groups were set up, a group without accelerant (control group: CK), a group with the addition of CQDs, and the remaining four groups with CQDs/PC(x). All AcoDMF reactors were placed in a constant temperature water bath at 36 ± 1 °C for 35 days. More details of the experimental setup can be found in the previous work [20].

Determination of the electron-donating capacities (EDC) and electron-accepting capacities (EAC) of CQDs (or PC) were measured by mediated electrochemical reduction (MER) and oxidation (MEO) methods [21]. EAC and EDC were measured in a three-electrode electrolytic cell system using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China). The working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode were glassy carbon electrode, graphite rod, and Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively. The electron shuttle solution used are diquat dibromide monohydrate (DQ, 11 mM) and 2,2′-amino-bis (3-ethyl-benzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS, 11 mM). Preparation of accelerant suspension: A certain mass of accelerant is added into the buffer solution (0.1 M KCl, 0.1 M K2HPO4, pH = 7) to prepare 4 mg/mL of accelerant suspension. The MER and MEO was analyzed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China) at a constant potential of −0.49 V and +0.61 V.

MER test: Firstly, 75 mL buffer solution was placed in a three-electrode system and N2 was used to remove oxygen from the buffer solution. Second, the electron shuttle solution (2 mL DQ) is added to the buffer solution and waits for a constant background current to recurred. Finally, a certain amount of accelerant (4 mg) was added into the buffer solution to test the response current curve of MER. After the constant background current recurred, the MER response current of different mass (8, 12, 16, and 20 mg) accelerant is tested.

Similarly, MEO was measured at a constant potential of +0.61 V with 2,2′-amino-bis (3-ethyl-benzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt as the electronic shuttle. The EAC and EDC are calculated as follows:

where Ired and Iox (μA) are the reductive and oxidative currents in MEO and MER, respectively; F = 96,485 s·A/mol e-, m (g) is the mass of the accelerant.

The electrochemical activity of the digestate was tested by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests [22], which also used a three-electrode system with graphite rods as the working electrode and counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Graphite rods (150 × Φ6 mm, 8–11 μΩm) were used as the working and counter electrodes, and Ag/AgCl electrode was used as a reference electrode. The fresh bulk solution from the digester was used as electrolyte for the three-electrode system. For the LSV test, the potential varied from −1.0 V to 0 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s; for the CV test, the supplied potential ranged from −0.9 V to 0.9 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

The biogas yield was expressed as a normalized volume of gas (P = 1 atm, T = 25 °C) measured by water displacement, divided by the VS added (CF + AR + AS). Biogas yields were analyzed using a modified Gompertz model that provides a estimation of biogas yields potential, maximum biogas yield rate, and lag time [23]:

where M is the cumulative biogas yields (mL/g VS), Hm is the biogas yields (mL/g VS), Rm is the maximum biogas yield rate (mL/g VS/d), T is the lag time (d), t is time (d), and e is a constant (2.718).

Chemical composition and morphology of CQDs and PC were determined using X-ray powder diffractometry (XRD, D/Max2200, USA), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X, USA), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, GeminiSEM 500, Germany). The surface functional groups of the CQDs and PC were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet iS50, USA). The CH4 and CO2 content were measured by gas chromatograph (GC9790, China). The contents of total solids (TS), volatile solids (VS), total chemical oxygen demand (TCOD), total alkalinity (TA), and total VFAs (TVFA) were measured according to the standard methods (APHA, 2005). The pH changes during the AcoD process were measured with a pH meter (PH-9701, China). Thermodynamic properties of digestate by thermal analyzer (TGA/DSC3, America). Total nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) were determined according to the standard methods (NY/T 525–2021).

The samples were collected after the digestion substrate in the AcoDMF reactor was uniformly mixed, and put into 50 mL sterile centrifuge tubes for low-temperature storage for DNA extraction. The V4 variable region of 16S rRNA was amplified by PCR using universal primers 349F (GTGYCAGCMGCCGCCGCTAA) and 806R (GGACTACNVGGGTWTCTAAT).
The experiment was repeated three times. Experimental results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation and evaluated by one-way analysis of variance.

It is observed from TEM images of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2
a) that the CQDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed with particle sizes less than 10 nm. The SEM image of PC (Fig. 2 b) shows a 3D interconnected structure with honeycomb-like pores. Its pore size ranges from tens of nanometers to a few microns. The synthesized CQDs have a distinct diffraction peak located near 2θ = 22.5° (Fig. 2 c), which was caused by the (002) lattice spacing of the amorphous carbon. Similar XRD patterns were reported in CQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method of aloe vera extract [24]. The XRD patterns of PC (Fig. 2 d) clearly show two broad peaks located near 22.5° and 44°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes, respectively. This indicated that PC is amorphous in structure [18]. These results demonstrated that the aloe rind-derived CQDs and PC were successfully prepared.

The high-resolution C1 s and O1 s spectra of CQDs and PC are shown in Fig. 2 e-g. The C1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 e) of CQDs is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.31 eV, 283.17, and 286.17, which are attributed to C = C, C − C, and C = O functional groups, respectively [24]. The C1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 f) of PC is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.61 eV, 286.08, and 287.98, which are attributed to C = C, C − O, and C = O functional groups, respectively [18,25]. CQDs and PC (Fig. 2 g) possessed two O-containing groups of C = O ((532.06 eV and 530.99 eV) and 531.53 eV) and C − O (533.26 eV and 532.88 eV) [18,25]. The C- and O-containing functional groups (C = O and O − C = O) have been proved to facilitate the charge transfer between acetogens and methanogens in the AD system, thereby enhancing the methanogenesis [18]. To clarify the surface functional groups of the CQDs and PC, the FTIR test was employed. FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 h) of CQDs and PC shows that the characteristic absorption bands are caused by the stretching vibrations of − OH (3419 and 3440 cm−1), C − H (2926 and 2923 cm−1), COO− ((1594 and 1402 cm−1) and (1628 and 1400 cm−1)), and C − O (1119 and 1120 cm−1) [18,19]. These findings confirmed the presence of C-, and O-containing functional groups in CQDs and PC sample, which is consistent with the results of XPS.

The mediated redox properties of CQDs and PC were evaluated by MER and MEO. Fig. 3
a-b shows the peak mediated reduction (oxidation) currents of CQDs and PC, from which it can be observed that the corresponding currents become more pronounced with increasing accelerant concentrations. The response current of CQDs is higher than that of PC at the same mass, indicating that CQDs have a better ability to mediate oxidation (reduction) compared to PC. Fig. 3 c-d shows the EAC and EDC of CQDs and PC. The EAC (or EDC) of CQDs and PC were 0.58 and 0.42 μmol e-/g (0.46 and 0.22 μmol e-/g), respectively. The CQDs have better EAC and EDC than PC, which may be caused by the reduction of functional groups when preparing PC at high temperatures [26]. In addition, CQDs and PC have good electron exchange capacity (EEC = EAC + EDC) of 1.04 and 0.64 μmol e-/g, respectively, which indicate that both carbon materials have the ability to potentially promote DIET process [26].

Biogas yield and gas composition were measured to assess the performance of the AcoDMF. From the daily gas production (Fig. 4
a), all AcoDMF show peaks around days 8, 16, and 25. The earliest peak may be due to the degradation of carbohydrates, and the remaining two peaks may be due to the degradation of crude protein and lignocellulose [27,28]. The maximum peaks for these three peaks were 36.8 (CQDs/PC(1.0)), 36.1 (CQDs/PC(1.0)), and 26.8 (CQDs) mL/g VS, respectively. In all experimental groups where accelerants were added, the peak value was higher than the CK, which indicates that adding CQDs and PC facilitates the conversion of organic matter into biogas. The CQDs and PC groups showed an earlier peak appearance than the CK, indicating that the accelerants accelerated biogas production.

Cumulative biogas production (Fig. 4 b) from AcoDMF of the CF with AR was influenced by different horizontal doses of accelerants. The AcoDMF with different doses of CQDs and PC resulted in higher cumulative biogas yields (587.0–648.5 mL/g VS) compared to CK (467.8 mL/g VS), with an increase of 25.5–38.6 %. It was also observed that cumulative biogas yield in the CQDs/PC group was higher (p < 0.05) than CQDs group, indicating that the CQDs coupled with PC promoted biogas yield. The highest cumulative biogas yield was obtained in the CQDs/PC(1.2) group. Fig. 4 b also shows the cumulative biogas yield from different AcoDMF that were fitted by a modified Gompertz model, with the relevant kinetic parameters shown in Table 2 . Overall, the model fitted the experimental data well with R2 values of 0.9973–0.9999. The fitting results showed that the CQDs/PC(1.2) group had a high biogas yield potential (Hm, 684.1 mL/g VS) and maximum biogas yield rate (Rm, 31.3 mL/g VS/d), which were 40.41 % and 29.9 % higher than the CK (487.2 mL/g VS, 24.1 mL/g VS/d), respectively. Added CQDs and PC had a beneficial effect on biogas yield whereas it had no effect on reducing the lag time. A similar phenomenon was found by Sun et al. [16].

In general, the produced biogas consists of CH4 (35–75 %), CO2 (15–55 %), and other gases [29]. The CH4 content (Fig. 4 c) of all the systems added with accelerants first increased and then remained relatively stable during the whole AcoDMF process. The highest CH4 content (64.5–67.6 %) in all groups with CQDs and PC was higher than the CK (52.3 %), suggesting that the addition of CQDs and PC facilitates the yield of CH4. The CO2 content of all experimental groups first decreased and then leveled off (Fig. 4 d). The methane yield of the CK under the MF (244.4 mL/g VS) was 21.5 %, higher than the methane increment of other anaerobic mono-digestion with the MF (11.4–12.5 %, Table 3 ), indicating that the AcoD under the MF was more effective in improving the gas production performance. CQDs/PC(1.2) group obtained higher (p < 0.05) cumulative methane yield than other CQDs/PC groups, and the methane production in CQDs/PC groups (402.9–438.4 mL/g VS) was significantly higher (p < 0.05) than that of CQDs group (378.6 mL/g VS). Methane production in CQDs/PC groups and CQDs group increased 64.7–79.2 % and 54.7 %, respectively, compared to the CK (244.7 mL/g VS), higher than the reported methane increment in other AcoD with carbon materials (1.7–49.7 %, Table 3), indicating that the MF coupled carbon accelerant (CQDs and PC) can improve the CH4 production performance of AD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH, TVFA, and TA and the removal efficiency of TCOD, TS, and VS
ce:section.ce:para:
A decrease in pH was observed at the beginning of the reaction and reached a minimum on the fifth day for all AcoDMF (Fig. 5
a). This was due to the hydrolysis of the substrate by microorganisms at the start of digestion to produce VFAs [43]. As the AcoD reaction proceeded, VFAs were used by anaerobic microorganisms to produce methane; thereafter, the pH gradually increased. The addition of appropriate concentrations of CQDs/PC facilitated faster recovery of the pH to favor methanogens growth compared with CK. This may be due to the enhanced AD performance of CQDs/PC, which leads to faster consumption of organic acids in system [3].

VFAs (Fig. 5 b) is an important intermediate in the AcoD process that directly affects the methanogen activity. Methanogen activity was inhibited when the concentration of TVFA in the AcoD was greater than 6 mg/mL [44]. TVFA concentrations increased in all AcoDMF treatments during the first five days, due to the production of VFAs by hydrolysis and acidogenesis, which coincided with varying reductions in the pH value. The group subjected to CQDs and PC produced more TVFA compared to the CK group, probably because the PC with its porous structure provided a suitable living environment for the hydrolyzing bacteria to perform better hydrolysis [45]. Owing to the biotransformation of TVFA, the concentration of TVFA decreased in all reactors. The TVFA of CQDs/PC(1.2) group was lower (p < 0.05) than that of other groups on day 10. The decrease was 2.3 mg/mL from days 5 to 10 in the CQDs/PC(1.2) group, which was significantly (p < 0.05) higher than the CK (1.8 mg/mL). This indicated that the CQDs and PC accelerated the consumption of TVFA.

TA (based CaCO3) is an indicator of a system's ability to resist acidification and is determined by acid-base titration reactions [46]. The changes in the TA for each group are shown in the inset of Fig. 5 b. The TA of AcoD systems with CQDs and PC were significantly (p＜0.05) higher than CK on day 5, and CQDs/PC(1.2) obtained higher (p < 0.05) TA on day 10 as compared with other CQDs/PC groups. This may be due to the degradation of proteins in the system caused by CQDs/PC [47]. Furthermore, the TVFA/TA ratio reflected the stability of the AcoD process. When the ratio was greater than 0.4, the AcoD process was unstable [48]. Fig. 5 c shows the TVFA/TA ratios with and without CQDs/PC at different concentrations. The increase in the TVFA/TA ratio during the first five days indicated the instability of the system in the early stages of AcoD. The TVFA/TA values changed rapidly from day 5 to day 10 in the CQDs/PC group than in the CK and CQDs groups, which indicated that CQDs coupled with PC were beneficial in improving the stability of AcoDMF [49].

After AcoD process, the TCOD (Fig. 5 d) of all groups decreased, and the TCOD of the CQDs/PC(1.2) group was lower (p < 0.05) than other groups, indicating that the organic matter was more completely consumed in the CQD/PC(1.2) group. This phenomenon may be attributed to the enrichment of anaerobic microorganisms after adding CQDs/PC. Sufficient anaerobic microorganisms were used to transform the organic matter into biogas [34]. The TCOD removal efficiency of CQDs/PC groups are 51.9 %−61.9 % (Fig. 5 d), 14.2 %−24.2 % and 4.1 %−14.1 % higher than CK (37.7 %) and CQDs group (47.8 %), respectively. This result suggests CQDs coupled with PC can promote organic matter degradation in AcoD. After batch AcoD, both TS and VS decreased and the CQDs/PC(1.2) group had the lowest (Fig. 5 e, f), which could also indicate that adding CQDs and PC facilitated organic matter degradation. The TS reduction rate (Fig. 5 e) of CK was 41.0 %. Comparatively, the TS reduction rate of the CQDs/PC groups increased by 2.34 % (CQDs), 3.8 % (CQDs/PC(0.8)), 7.2 % (CQDs/PC(1.0)), 13.7 % (CQDs/PC(1.2)), and 9.6 % (CQDs/PC(1.4)), respectively. The VS reduction rate (Fig. 5 f) for the CQDs/PC groups ranged from 45.1 % to 55.6 % while it was only 40.8 % in the CK. The TS and VS removal rates of CQDs/PC group were significantly higher (p＜0.05) than the CK. Overall, CQDs/PC can increase the consumption and decomposition of organic matter, which can be contributed to the fact that the addition of CQDs/PC can facilitate the hydrolysis-acidification of AcoDMF and enrich microorganisms [3,15].

The thermogravimetric (TG) curve can reflect the mass loss of digestate in the process of thermochemical transformation, and the differential thermogravimetry (DTG) curve can more clearly show the devolatilization stage of digestate [50]. As shown in Fig. 6
a-b, the pyrolysis process of the digestate can be divided into three stages. The first stage of weight loss was mainly due to the volatilization of water and the removal of low molecular weight hydrocarbons, which occurred at 50–180 °C [51]. At this stage, the weight loss of the CQDs/PC(1.2) group (4.4 %, Fig. 6 c) was lower than CK (4.7 %), indicating that the amount of low molecular weight organic matter was reduced in the AcoDMF with CQDs/PC. The second stage occurs at 180–550 °C. During this stage, the weight loss is attributed to the pyrolysis of protein, hemicellulose, and cellulose [47]. In the second stage, the weight loss of the CQDs/PC(1.2) group and the CK were 35.8 % and 40.0 % (Fig. 6 c) respectively, indicating that the addition of CQDs and PC was conducive to the decomposition and digestion of proteins, hemicellulses, and cellulose in the AcoDMF. The third stage (550–900 °C) was mainly due to the mass loss caused by the pyrolysis of refractory organic matter [52]. Compared with CK, the digestate from the AcoDMF with CQDs and PC showed a smaller weight loss (Fig. 6 c). Compared with CQDs in previous work [19], CQDs/PC(1.2) improved the mass residue of digestates during thermogravimetric process. This result indicated that CQDs coupled with PC can promote the degradation of refractory organic matter in AcoDMF.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curve (Fig. 6 d) show the heat release of the digestate. Two obvious heat release peaks correspond to the two peaks in the second stage of DTG. After normalization, the exotherm of digestate in the CK and CQDs/PC(1.2) groups was 1435.5 and 1389.1 J/g, respectively. This result further indicated that addition of CQDs and PC was conducive to the digestion of organic matter in AcoDMF. Overall, the host and guest assembly accelerant (CQDs and PC) was beneficial for organic matter degradation in AcoDMF.

The NPK content of the digestate is shown in Table 4 . Firstly, according to the Chinese agricultural standard (NY/T 525–2021), the pH value of fertilizer is suitable between 5.5 and 8.5, and the pH values at the end of AcoD in this work are within this range. Second, the total nutrient content needs to be greater than 4.0 %. The total nutrient content of CQD/PC(1.2) group digestate was 4.9 %, and the N, P, and K contents were 21.4, 14.0, and 13.9 g/kg, respectively. The TN content is higher than bio-organic fertilizer. However, the TP and TK content are higher than soil but lower than bio-organic fertilizer, so the digestate need be composited with P- and K-rich fertilizers, which can reduce the use of traditional chemical fertilizers.

The AcoD efficiency depends mainly on the interactions between anaerobic microorganisms, which satisfy their energy requirements by exchanging electrons [53]. Compared to the CK, adding CQDs/PC(1.2) resulted higher redox peaks and the relatively larger area of CV integral area (Fig. 7
a). The larger the area of the CV curve, the better the charging and discharging performance, and the significantly higher (p＜0.05) the current peak (CQDs/PC(1.2) group: −0.74 mA; CK: −0.51 mA), the stronger the redox capability [53]. It was observed in the LSV curves (Fig. 7 b) that the CQDs/PC(1.2) at the same voltage (e.g., −0.84 V) had a stronger response current (absolute value: 0.31 mA) than the CK (0.28 mA; p＜0.05), indicating that the conductivity of the AcoD system was improved in the presence of CQDs and PC. Overall, the AcoD with CQDs/PC(1.2) had good charging and discharging properties, redox characteristics, and electrical conductivity, suggesting that the AcoD with CQDs/PC had a faster interspecies electron transfer rate.

The effect of accelerants on the bacterial community of AcoD was confirmed, as illustrated in Fig. 7 c. The Firmicutes, Priteobacteria, and Bacteroidetes were the dominant phylum in two groups. Firmicutes was a well-known bacterium capable of degrading carbohydrates, proteins, and fats to produce VFAs [54]. The CQDs/PC(1.2) group had a higher relative abundance (41.6 %) than the CK (29.3 %), indicating that the CQDs coupled with PC favored the enrichment of the Firmicutes. The relative abundance of Priteobacteria and Bacteroidetes in CQDs/PC(1.2) group (23.0 % and 23.1 %) did not change much compared to those of CK (26.0 % and 22.3 %). Priteobacteria can degrade the VFAs produced in the hydrolysis stage into acetate used by methanogens [55]. The Bacteroidetes produces various enzymes (cellulases, proteases, and etc.) during AD to dissolve the substrate of the AD into sugars and amino acids [55]. The sum of the abundance of these three bacteria was enhanced in the CQDs/PC(1.2) group (87.8 %) compared to the CK (77.6 %), indicating that the CQDs coupled with PC facilitated the enrichment of hydrolytic bacteria. Fig. 7 d shows the structure of archaea. The relative abundance of Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, Methanosarcina, and Pacearchaeota Incertae Sedis AR13 in CQDs/PC(1.2) group (27.6 %, 10.4 %, 11.2 %, and 12.8 %) rose compared to those of CK (24.7 %, 8.4 %, 7.1 %, and 0.2 %). During AcoD, Methanobacterium and Pacearchaeota Incertae Sedis AR13 were converting CO2 to CH4 by using H2 as an electron donor, and their increased abundance may indicate that hybrid accelerants can enhance hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis [56]. Methanomassiliicoccus was methylotrophic methanogen, indicating that CQDs/PC could enhance the methylotrophic methanogenic pathway in AcoD [57]. Methanosarcina was a multipathway methanogenic that can produce CH4 via H2/CO2, acetic, and methyl compounds [57]. Also, Methanobacterium and Methanosarcina have the ability to stimulate DIET, and their increased abundance indicate that the CQDs coupled with PC promotes the enrichment of DIET-associated bacteria [3]. In short, CQDs coupled PC promoted the enrichment of hydrolytic bacteria in the AcoD and enriched methanogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding of the enhanced methanogenesis in AcoD with CQDs and PC
ce:section.ce:para: The construction of DIET using carbon materials is a common method for enhancing methane production in AD. In this work, the addition of CQDs and PC to AcoD improved organic matter degradation and resulted in improved CH4 yields. Host-guest assembly accelerant CQDs/PC promoted the CH4 yields of AcoD for three reasons.

Firstly, the positive effects of CQDs and PC on substrate decomposition were confirmed. The addition of CQDs and PC led to enhanced hydrolysis of the substrate, as demonstrated by the changes in TVFA (Fig. 5 b), TCOD removal efficiency (Fig. 5 d), and enrichment of hydrolytic bacteria (Fig. 7 c); this facilitated the availability of the substrate to methanogenic bacteria, which ultimately led to an increased CH4 production. Furthermore, when compared with the control group of a previous study, it was observed that the MF coupled with carbon accelerants (CQDs and PC) promoted the breakdown of organic matter in AcoD [18]. Secondly, guest materials PC could improve the digestive environment and microorganism growth. PC are carbon materials with large specific surface areas that can adsorb harmful substances in the system and mitigate the impact of stochastic processes (i.e., in terms of environmental conditions) on microbial growth [58]. The porous structure of PC was conducive to the fixation and growth of microorganisms [3]. CQDs coupled with PC enriched the anaerobic microorganisms in the microbial community (Fig. 7 c, d), thus enhancing the AcoD performance.

In general, the MF coupled with host–guest assembly carbon accelerant (CQDs and PC) can enhance the decomposition of substrates, provide growth sites for microorganisms, and establish electrical connections between the microorganisms, ultimately enhancing the DIET process and promoting methane production.

The host–guest assembly accelerant CQDs/PC improved the AcoD performance of cattle feces and aloe rind. The highest cumulative methane yield (438.4 mL/g VS), TCOD removal efficiency (61.9 %), and good total nutrient content (4.9 %) and low thermogravimetric loss (45.5 %) of digestates were obtained for the CQDs/PC(1.2) group. CQDs/PC enhanced the electron transport capacity of the AcoD system and enriched Methanobacterium and Methanosarcina colonies. CQDs and PC facilitated electron transfer between anaerobic microorganisms.
